channel.title: Feedly Digest
channel.description: Current article list from the Feedly digest on maraz.sk.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] What Bazel taught us about Terraform
item.description:
Stategraph frames Terraform planning as an incremental build-system problem: complete input tracking, persistent dependency graphs, and fine-grained locks are what make caching and parallelism safe.

  • Speed depends on correctness because caches only work when every input is known and hashed.
  • A persistent HCL dependency graph enables affected-subgraph planning instead of rebuilding the whole graph for every run.
  • Sandboxing is a correctness tool: missing dependencies should fail loudly rather than silently poisoning cached output.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Type out the code
item.description:
The essay argues that AI-era programmers still need precise recall of syntax, schemas, names, and APIs because weak fluency makes it harder to reason, review, and avoid duplicate code.

  • Typing code from memory builds the same generation effect that improves comprehension in other learning domains.
  • Agents do not remove the need to know local types, functions, imports, and project vocabulary.
  • Generated tests still need human scrutiny because self-contained trivial tests can validate the wrong thing.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Learn the Shape of Your Data
item.description:
The piece shows how Rust strings, Go slices, and similar data structures become easier to debug once you reason about inline values, pointers, ownership, allocation, and copying.

  • Rust ownership feels less mystical when a String is understood as a small header that owns heap bytes.
  • Go slice behavior follows from the descriptor and backing-array split.
  • Data layout is a practical debugging and performance tool, not a compiler-engineer detour.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Fixing Every Bug
item.description:
The thread surfaces the hidden cost of policies that require every filed bug to be fixed by severity SLA, even when the defect has low value or no realistic priority.

  • Severity-based SLAs need explicit defer, acknowledge-only, or won't-fix outcomes.
  • Customer-service goals and engineering-capacity goals conflict unless prioritization is owned above the team.
  • A blanket fix-every-bug policy can make triage look objective while hiding opportunity cost.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Is it normal for a senior to have to spend so much time wrangling other teams for availability and approvals?
item.description:
The discussion captures a common senior-engineer shift: cross-org delivery often becomes dependency management, escalation, and making approval latency visible rather than pure implementation.

  • Missed design reviews and slow approvals are process ownership failures, not just personal inconvenience.
  • Senior scope often includes turning invisible dependency drag into visible risk.
  • Escalation paths, written decision records, and response SLAs matter when informal pings stop working.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] The just-say-no engineer was a ZIRP phenomenon
item.description:
Sean Goedecke argues that broad engineering gatekeeping was easier to justify when companies had excess capacity; AI amplified the pressure, but post-ZIRP economics changed the incentives.

  • High-bar gatekeeping is still valuable in core infrastructure and pure engineering domains.
  • Feature-experiment environments now reward controlled throughput more than broad resistance to code volume.
  • AI is not the only force weakening the just-say-no posture; capital discipline changed what organizations value.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] The Tacit Dimension: Why Your Best Engineers Can't Tell You What They Know
item.description:
The article uses tacit knowledge to explain why AI tools can follow explicit docs while still missing unwritten codebase conventions, historical constraints, and senior review judgment.

  • AI assistance over-indexes on artifacts that can be written down and misses team-specific implicit knowledge.
  • Fluent code explanations are not evidence of grounded understanding of a particular codebase.
  • Pairing, review, and senior translation work preserve system knowledge that docs and tests cannot fully encode.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Using algebra to verify a flight-plan bug fix in Lean
item.description:
A real air-traffic-control bug fix became tractable in Lean only after the author replaced low-level index-heavy specs with an algebraic path/category model that made proof obligations explicit.

  • Specs over duplicated route identifiers need positional structure, not set membership.
  • LLMs were weak at inventing clean specs but effective at routine proof work once the abstraction was right.
  • A simple optimization can trigger substantial proof obligations when correctness is formalized.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] 314 npm packages just got compromised, 271 @antv, echarts-for-react, size-sensor, timeago.js
item.description:
SafeDep documents a large npm supply-chain compromise that combined malicious semver-resolved package versions, preinstall hooks, GitHub-hosted payload delivery, CI token abuse, and local AI-agent persistence.

  • Semver ranges could install malicious higher versions even when the latest dist-tag did not move.
  • The payload targeted CI secrets, GitHub Actions OIDC token exchange, npm publish tokens, local Codex and Claude hooks, VS Code folder-open tasks, and Docker socket escape.
  • Useful IOCs include atool-published packages from 2026-05-19 01:44 to 02:06 UTC, bun run index.js preinstall hooks, and .claude or .vscode setup files.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Protocols for transactional usage of object storage
item.description:
The article explains how databases can build serializable transactional protocols over object storage by combining atomic writes, conditional writes, consistent listing, manifests, and careful garbage collection.

  • Conditional writes turn object metadata into a compare-and-set primitive for coordinating writers.
  • Sequenced filenames reduce conflict cost and preserve history, but require explicit garbage-collection safety.
  • Age-based deletion alone can create branched histories when stalled writers resume, so fencing or boundary files are needed.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Simulating Infinity in Conway's Game of Life with Modern C++
item.description:
The GOLDE writeup walks through a high-performance HashLife implementation using canonical immutable quadtrees, arena allocation, cached future states, and topology abstraction.

  • Canonical immutable quadtrees avoid recomputing identical subpatterns.
  • Arena allocation gives pointer stability and better locality for the memoized node graph.
  • Arbitrary-generation stepping can be decomposed into power-of-two jumps while bounded worlds reuse an algorithm designed for an infinite universe.

item.title: [Philosophy] Pluralistic: There's no such thing as "age verification" (19 May 2026)
item.description:
Cory Doctorow treats age verification as a failed identity-attribution architecture: it creates surveillance and breach surfaces, is easy to evade, and pushes policy toward broader network control.

  • Age verification becomes internet-wide identity binding rather than a narrow safety check.
  • VPN evasion is predictable, so enforcement pressure escalates toward traffic and platform control.
  • Requirements that ignore adversarial adaptation produce new mandates, not working systems.
